---
title: "Exercise: Reverse complement"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Write `reverse_complement(seq: &[u8]) -> Vec<u8>`: given a DNA sequence as a byte slice, return a newly-allocated [`Vec<u8>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html) containing the complement of each base in reverse order.

This is the first exercise where the function **owns** the data it returns. The caller is given a fresh `Vec<u8>` they can mutate, store, or drop as they wish — the function keeps nothing.

## Background

The reverse complement is one of the most common operations in bioinformatics. To compute it, complement each base (A↔T, C↔G) and reverse the order. Aliasing on day 1: `ATGCATGC` → complemented `TACGTACG` → reversed `GCATGCAT`. We will reuse the [`complement_base`](../day1/02-complement.qmd) function from day 1.

![Reverse complement of `ATTGC`: reverse the order, then complement each base. Doing it in the opposite order — complement first, then reverse — produces the same result, because the two operations commute.](images/reverse-complement.svg){#fig-revcomp fig-alt="Three rows of five monospace boxes. Row 1: A T T G C (input). A left-pointing arrow labelled 'reverse' connects to row 2: C G T T A. A down arrow labelled 'complement (A↔T, C↔G)' connects to row 3: G C A A T, highlighted in light yellow with an orange border to mark the final output."}

## The exercise

```bash
cd day2/ex-reverse-complement
```

Make all seven tests pass.

## How to run

```bash
cargo test
cargo run -- ATGCATGC    # expected: GCATGCAT
cargo run -- AAAA        # expected: TTTT
cargo run -- ACGT        # expected: ACGT  (this DNA sequence is a palindrome)
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — allocate the right-sized Vec up front"}
[`Vec::with_capacity(n)`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html#method.with_capacity) creates an empty vector that has already reserved space for `n` items. Compared to [`Vec::new()`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html#method.new), this avoids the vector having to grow (and reallocate) as you push into it:

```rust
let mut out: Vec<u8> = Vec::with_capacity(seq.len());
```

You know exactly how big the result will be — same length as the input — so allocate exactly that much.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — iterating from the end"}
A [range](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/expressions/range-expr.html) like `0..seq.len()` walks indices forward. To walk them backward, call [`.rev()`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/iter/trait.DoubleEndedIterator.html#method.rev) on it:

```rust
for i in (0..seq.len()).rev() {
    // i goes from seq.len()-1 down to 0
}
```

On day 3 we will write the same loop more compactly with [`seq.iter().rev().map(...).collect()`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/iter/trait.Iterator.html#method.rev), but the indexed loop is fine for today and easier to read while learning.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — pushing onto the buffer"}
[`Vec::push`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html#method.push) appends a single element:

```rust
out.push(complement_base(seq[i]));
```

When the loop ends, the bare name `out` (no `;`) is your return value.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
fn reverse_complement(seq: &[u8]) -> Vec<u8> {
    let mut out: Vec<u8> = Vec::with_capacity(seq.len());
    for i in (0..seq.len()).rev() {
        out.push(complement_base(seq[i]));
    }
    out
}
```

Full reference in `day2/ex-reverse-complement-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Next exercise (02)**: rewrite this so the caller passes in a buffer to fill, instead of allocating a fresh `Vec` on every call. That is how every fast bioinformatics tool actually does it in its inner loop.
- **Day 3**: rewrite the body as one iterator chain — `seq.iter().rev().map(|&b| complement_base(b)).collect()`.
